intro:
Judea (Greek Ioudaia, Latin Judaea) is the name for the region of southern Palestine corresponding to the old tribal territories of Judah and Benjamin. The name derives from Judah; after the Babylonian exile the returning remnant, centred on Jerusalem, gave it to the restored province (Haggai 1:1; Ezra 5:8). Its heartland was the Judean hill country, the wilderness sloping to the Dead Sea, and the Shephelah, with Jerusalem, Bethlehem, Hebron, and Jericho as its chief towns.

Politically Judea passed through many hands. Under Persia it was a small temple-state; after Alexander it fell to the Ptolemies and Seleucids; the Maccabean revolt won a period of Jewish independence; and in 63 BC Rome took control, ruling through Herod the Great and then, after his son Archelaus was deposed, through Roman governors (procurators) such as Pontius Pilate (Luke 3:1). In this era “Judea” could mean the narrow district or, more broadly, the whole land of the Jews.

Judea is a primary stage of the Gospel story. Jesus was born in Bethlehem of Judea in the days of Herod the king (Matthew 2:1); John the Baptist preached in the Judean wilderness (Matthew 3:1); and it was in Judea, at Jerusalem, that Jesus was tried, crucified, and raised. The Gospels often set Judea alongside Galilee and Samaria as the main regions of the land (John 4:3-4).

After Pentecost Judea was the cradle of the church. The risen Lord’s commission charted the gospel’s spread outward — “in Jerusalem and in all Judea and Samaria, and to the end of the earth” (Acts 1:8) — and when persecution scattered the Jerusalem believers “throughout the regions of Judea and Samaria,” the word went with them (Acts 8:1). The churches of Judea became the mother congregations whose faith and sufferings Paul held up as an example (1 Thessalonians 2:14). Judea thus stands as the homeland of the Messiah and the birthplace of the Christian movement.

link_tables.title: Places

cells: Jerusalem
City of David and the temple; where Christ died and rose (2 Sam 5:7)
Bethlehem
Town of David, where the Messiah was born (Mic 5:2)
Hebron
David's first royal city and patriarchal burial place (2 Sam 5:5)
Bethany
Home of Mary, Martha, and Lazarus near Jerusalem (Jn 11:1)
Jericho
City of palms where Zacchaeus met Jesus (Lk 19:1)
Emmaus
Where the risen Lord was known in the breaking of bread (Lk 24:13)
Lachish
Judean fortress-city besieged by Sennacherib (2 Kgs 18:14)

link_tables.note: Click any name to open its article. Markers for these places and events appear on the map above.

cells: The Babylonian Captivity
Judah's deportation when Jerusalem fell in 586 BC (2 Kgs 25:11)
David
Shepherd-king who made Jerusalem his capital (2 Sam 5:7)
Solomon
His son who built the temple in Jerusalem (1 Kgs 6:1)
Hezekiah
King who trusted God against Assyria (2 Kgs 18:5)
Josiah
King whose reform restored the Book of the Law (2 Kgs 22:8)
Herod the Great
Roman client king who rebuilt the temple (Mt 2:1)
Jesus Christ
Born in Bethlehem, crucified and raised at Jerusalem (Mt 2:1)
John the Baptist
Forerunner who preached in the Judean wilderness (Mt 3:1)
Mary
Mother of Jesus, who bore him at Bethlehem (Lk 2:7)
Pontius Pilate
Roman governor who sentenced Jesus to the cross (Lk 3:1)
Babylonia
Destroyed Jerusalem and the temple in 586 BC (2 Kgs 25:9)
Persia
Restored the exiles and the temple-province (Ezra 1:2)
Rome
Ruled Judea through Herod and the governors (Lk 3:1)
